note:
Ken Harlan是MobileFuse的创始人兼首席执行官，基于U.S.在应用程序的广告和DOOH平台，提供跨零售，食品和饮料，餐厅，旅游，政府和医疗保健的领先组织。
  实际上，软件开发套件（SDK）集成是为什么这些整形单位公司拥有如此突出的存在的关键组成部分。出于参考，SDK是一组软件开发工具，库，代码示例，进程和指南，帮助开发人员创建或增强所构建的应用程序。
  通过数字营销镜头，SDK提供了应用程序内分析，对广告系列测试，归属信息，位置详细信息，货币化功能等有所了解。在谷歌和Facebook等公司的情况下，他们能够以他们的数据和达到的数据和达到燕尾榫提供这些洞察力。
 虽然这确实为开发人员和出版商提供了有用的能力，但它也会延续有助于他们所感知的垄断状况的因素 - 而且缺乏竞争诉讼的损害。
 几乎所有（90％）广告批准的Android应用程序都有Google的Admob SDK集成，来自Statista的数据显示。此外，Facebook观众网络SDK有19％的全球Android应用程序利用移动广告。值得注意的是，这两个玩家外面的大多数替代方案“领先”广告SDKS在Android应用程序中使用不到13％的时间。
 由于应用程序生态系统迅速扩展到移动，App开发商和出版商将从识别更多的SDK来识别经济和安全的方式，从而迅速扩展。
  虽然当今市场上有许多可用的SDK，但一些关键因素有助于谷歌和Facebook的整体支配地位。最基本的是各自的组织的范围和行业卓越。但是，这里的更大组件是缺乏资源和时间应用程序开发人员。
note_en:
Ken Harlan is the founder and CEO of  MobileFuse, a U.S.-based. in-app advertising and DOOH platform that serves leading organizations across retail, food and beverage, restaurant, tourism, government, and healthcare.
  In reality, software development kit (SDK) integrations are a critical component of why these monolith companies have such a prominent presence. For reference, an SDK is a set of software development tools, libraries, code samples, processes and guides that help developers create or enhance the apps they’re building.
  Through a digital marketing lens, SDKs provide in-app analytics, insights on campaign testing, attribution information, location details, monetization capabilities and more. In the case of companies like Google and Facebook, their ability to provide these insights dovetails with their data and reach.
 While that does deliver useful capabilities to developers and publishers alike, it also perpetuates the factors contributing to their perceived monopolistic status — and the detriments a lack of competition fosters.
 Almost all (90%) ad-monetized Android apps have Google’s Admob SDK integrated,  data from Statista showed. Additionally, the Facebook Audience Network SDK is present in 19% of all global Android apps utilizing mobile ads. It’s worth noting that the large majority of alternative “leading” advertising SDKs outside these two players are used less than 13% of the time in Android apps.
 As the app ecosystem rapidly expands beyond the borders of mobile, app developers and publishers would benefit immensely from identifying economical and secure ways of adopting more SDKs.
  While there are many SDKs available in the market today, a few key factors contribute to Google and Facebook’s overall dominance. The most basic is around the respective organizations’ reach and industry notoriety. However, a larger component here is the lack of resources and time app developers have.
title: 关于SDK集成的真相及其对开发人员的影响
title_en: The truth about SDK integrations and their impact on developers

  “我的女儿认为，我为他们工作而言是有点酷，”Epi​​c的律师回应了。 Gonzalez Rogers法官提供了她自己的儿子作为反驳。
  “他想玩电子游戏，”56岁的法官说。 “我告诉他”不“这么多倍，他创造了自己的创造，他做了很少的牌，他会把他放入袖子，所以他可以玩游戏。他现在是一个航空工程师，我很高兴我说'没有'“ 
在德克萨斯州提出，法官冈萨雷斯罗杰斯参加了普林斯顿大学，工作周末清洁房屋和切割草，以帮助支付学费。
  她赢得了德克萨斯大学，奥斯汀法学院的法律学位，并在旧金山的Cooley LLP中度过了早期的职业生涯。 2008年，然后 - 加利福尼亚州政府队Arnold Schwarzenegger指定了她到阿拉米达县的高级法院。 2011年，当时 - 总统巴拉克奥巴马在苹果公司，谷歌，Facebook和Twitter的一个小时之内提升了她的联邦替补席
  她的袖会是技术沉重的，但变化。去年，她制造了头条新闻，旨在腾出奥巴马时代气候监管的特朗普管理回滚。早期，她举行了Abercrombie＆amp;惠誉通过要求她去除她的头巾来非法歧视穆斯林员工。青少年服装零售商表示，其着装代码对其品牌形象至关重要，但同意在裁决后改变其“神灵政策”。
    第九次诉讼上诉法院向一名被指控卖给卧底联邦代理人的人提供了向被指控的人授予家庭拘留的决定。法官Gonzalez Rogers允许他留在祖母上，要求他花费至少一个半天和半天的时间做书籍报告，就困难的情况克服了困难的人的故事。
  一路上，贾祖尔·罗杰斯法官在诉讼人中获得了声誉，因为没有顽固的法学家。根据法院成绩单的说法，她已经责备了她发现不尊重的掠夺和面部表情的诉讼剂。
  2018年，企业律师要求延长申请截止日期。他的借口是预定的脚踝手术。法官不如交感神经。 “如果你不喜欢我的日程安排，请去一个地方法官，”她说。 “他们对你来说比我会更好。”
  其他人见过一个温暖的一面。虽然没有批准午餐休息，但她是零食的信徒，喂养陪伴巧克力芯片饼干 - 面团松饼，被她的儿子烤，由她的母亲制作的松饼和贸易商乔的零食。 
“她对法律采取了真诚而不是那么闷闷的兴趣，但它是令人难以置信的人类，”旧金山保罗哈斯廷斯LLP的反托拉斯律师托马斯P. Brown说。
    在史诗诉苹果审判，法官冈萨雷斯罗杰斯已经表示愿意跳进询问证人，不得不为史诗首席执行官蒂姆·斯威尼几个尖锐的。在一次交换中，她驳回了Sweeney先生，因为她更改变了一个关于他会丢失的案子的思想更多的问题：“你的备份计划是什么？”
  斯威伊先生似乎没有准备好。 “如果苹果的行为是合法的，那么我承认苹果将有权以任何原因从开发人员计划中删除史诗，而且没有理由，然后苹果可以决定，”他说。
  她的视频名人的立场可能正在不断发展。她指出，她的女儿有一个任天堂开关。去年的听证会，史诗的律师正试图让iPhone与视频游戏机相比的可移植性是独一无二的，当时她被打断了。
  “你忘记了开关，”她说。 “我昨天上线，至少在湾区，你无法在任何地方开关。”
note_en:
U.S. District Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers says she abhors when lawyers use hyperbole and grandstand like they are Perry Mason. She says lunch breaks are the enemy of productivity and that parents ought to observe  Pi Day by feeding children pies. And she says she helped her son find success as an aeronautical engineer by denying him videogames.
  Now, the tech world is waiting to hear the biggest pronouncement of Judge Gonzalez Rogers’s career when she decides whether  Apple    AAPL  -1.88%  grip over the distribution and payment of apps on its mobile devices violates antitrust law. At stake is Epic Games Inc.’s “Fortnite,” a videogame with hundreds of millions of players and control of the sprawling mobile app marketplace.
  “Everyone wants to read her mind,” said Chris Sagers, a Cleveland State University law professor and an antitrust scholar.
  The conflict erupted after Epic Games introduced an in-app payment system within “Fortnite” that skirted Apple commissions and its policy requiring developers to use its own payment processing services. That prompted Apple to  expel “Fortnite” from the App Store, provoking  Epic’s antitrust lawsuit and its claims that Apple is exploiting the popularity of its devices to squeeze developers.
    Apple says that it isn’t a monopoly and that competition is flourishing in a distribution landscape that includes Google Android software and gaming consoles. The  trial, which kicked off last week, is expected to run through May.
    Neither Apple nor “Fortnite” maker Epic are newcomers in Judge Gonzalez Rogers’s courtroom in Oakland, Calif.
  In 2014, she presided over a lawsuit that accused Apple of suppressing competition for its iPod music players. A  jury sided with Apple.
  Apple later prevailed in another case before Judge Gonzalez Rogers. She declined to certify a class-action accusing Apple and  AT&T of unlawfully preventing iPhone customers from switching carriers.
  “She seems to keep ruling in favor of Apple,” said Prof. Sagers, but he and other scholars caution against reading too much into the earlier cases and rulings.
  Judge Gonzalez Rogers has hinted in the past that she is willing to at least entertain an antitrust theory crucial to Epic’s legal argument: that a company can wield excessive power in the market for its own products and services, according to John Newman, a University of Miami antitrust law professor. Courts reviewing antitrust complaints seldom draw the boundaries of a market as narrowly as Epic is by claiming that Apple is illegally  restraining trade within its own Apple ecosystem.
  Just weeks ago, Judge Gonzalez Rogers was clued into the “Fortnite” craze while hearing a would-be class-action suit against Epic that ultimately settled.
  At a 2019 hearing, a puzzled Judge Gonzalez Rogers asked the lawyers: “Is ‘Fortnite’ as big a deal as they’re saying it is?”
  “My daughters think it’s kind of cool that I get to work for them,” Epic’s lawyer responded. Judge Gonzalez Rogers offered her own son as a rebuttal.
  “He wanted to play videogames,” the 56-year-old judge said. “I told him ‘no’ so many times that out of paper, he created his own and he made little cards that he would put into the sleeve so he could play games. He’s now an aeronautical engineer, and I am quite happy that I said ‘no.’ ”
  Raised in Texas, Judge Gonzalez Rogers attended Princeton University, working weekends cleaning houses and cutting grass to help pay tuition.
  She earned her law degree from University of Texas, Austin School of Law and spent her early career as a litigator at Cooley LLP in San Francisco. In 2008, then-California Gov. Arnold Schwarzenegger appointed her to the Alameda County Superior Court. In 2011, then-President Barack Obama elevated her to the federal bench, with headquarters within an hour’s drive of Apple, Google,  Facebook and  Twitter
  Her docket is tech-heavy but varied. Last year she made headlines with a sharply worded decision vacating a Trump administration rollback of an Obama-era climate regulation. Early on, she held that  Abercrombie & Fitch   unlawfully  discriminated against a Muslim employee by requiring her to remove her head scarf. The teen-apparel retailer said its dress code was essential to its brand image  but agreed to change its “look policy” after the ruling.
    The Ninth U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals overruled her decision to grant home detention to a man accused of trying to sell a grenade launcher to an undercover federal agent. Judge Gonzalez Rogers had allowed him to stay with his grandmother with a requirement that he spend at least an hour and a half a day doing book reports on stories of people who overcame difficult circumstances.
  Along the way, Judge Gonzalez Rogers has gained a reputation among litigators as a jurist not to be trifled with. She has scolded litigators for gesticulations and facial expressions she found disrespectful, according to court transcripts.
  In 2018, a corporate lawyer asked to extend a filing deadline. His excuse was a scheduled ankle surgery. The judge was less than sympathetic. “If you don’t like my schedule, go to a magistrate judge,” she said. “They will be a lot nicer to you than I will.”
  Others have seen a warmer side. Though not approving of lunch breaks, she is a believer in snacks, feeding jurors chocolate-chip cookie-dough muffins baked by her son, muffins made by her mother and treats from Trader Joe’s.
  “She takes a sincere and not-so-stuffy interest in the law, but it’s incredibly human,” said Thomas P. Brown, an antitrust lawyer at Paul Hastings LLP in San Francisco.
    In the Epic v. Apple trial, Judge Gonzalez Rogers has shown a willingness to jump into questioning witnesses and had several sharp ones for Epic Chief Executive Tim Sweeney. In one exchange, she had dismissed Mr. Sweeney only to stop as she changed her mind with one more question about what he would do if he lost the case: “What is your backup plan?”
  Mr. Sweeney seemed unprepared. “If Apple’s actions are lawful, then I acknowledge Apple would have the right to remove Epic from the developer program for any reason or no reason, and then it would be up to Apple to decide,” he said.
  Her stance on videogames may be evolving. She has noted that her daughter has a Nintendo Switch. At a hearing last year, a lawyer for Epic was trying to make the point that the iPhone was unique for its portability compared with videogame consoles, when she interrupted.
  “You’re forgetting the Switch,” she said. “I went online yesterday, and, at least, in the Bay Area, you can’t get a Switch anywhere.”
title: yvonne gonzalez rogers的剖面档案，他是史诗般的v。苹果审判并以前处理了两家涉及两家公司的案例
title_en: Profile of Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers, who is presiding over the Epic v. Apple trial and has previously handled several cases involving both companies

note:
上周，史诗六的法官询问ePIC是否真的对Apple有一个反托拉斯案，或者是否只想帮助孩子们进行冲动购买。 yvonneGonzalez罗杰斯法官正在谈论人们为他们的应用支付的地方以及如何为他们的应用支付的重要性，今天她继续询问那种询问，这表明一种史诗从未最初放在桌子上的应用商店政策变化。
 史诗起诉苹果从iOS禁止富人，在一个直接支付系统的V-Bucks，Fortnite的游戏中的货币。史诗称为不公平和垄断。但苹果认为，即使以折扣价格，它允许开发人员通过Safari浏览器销售应用内购买 - 所以没有锁定。虽然Epic本身专注于解释为什么Web应用程序不是替代本地人的替代品，但它的专家证人David Evans提出了另一个主要问题：反向指导规则。
 反转向规则（在此上下文中）请参阅禁止开发人员从指向Apple生态系统以外的用户的规则。 iOS开发人员无法添加链接或参考，告诉人们在其网站上获得更好的交易，或将电子邮件发送给通过Apple创建的帐户。 Android也有这些规则，但它延迟了它们的严重镇压，直到它可以在Android上安装第三方商店和Sideload应用程序，谷歌生态系统的开发人员通常有更多的选择。
  埃文斯是一个经济学家，最初是通过将Apple与优步相比进行比较来解释应用程序内购买，将App Developer与Uber驱动程序进行比较，他们与客户建立了良好的关系。客户希望直接雇用驾驶员，但乘车公司（代表苹果公司）要求客户通过其应用支付。
 罗杰斯法官似乎并没有说服。通过浏览器购买V-Bucks，她注意到，似乎是一个直接支付司机的乘客。 “鉴于您的优步示例，该分发过程没有任何影响。”
 埃文斯基本回应说，在这个类比中，驾驶室司机甚至不能相当于给予乘客他们的电话号码。 “epic无法留言iOS应用程序用户并告诉他们”你可以去网络并更便宜地获得这种情况。“或者'我真的鼓励你去网络并获得V-Bucks，'”埃文斯反对。他说，问题是要求EPIC使用Apple支付处理的结合加上“整套障碍”，使其更加难以告诉用户他们有另一种选择。
 这些反向指导规定在审判之前提出 - 瑜伽应用程序制造商瑜伽佛陀上周抱怨他们。但这一次，罗杰斯提供了一个明显的后续问题。如果没有反向指导规定，她问过，史诗仍然有苹果的系统存在问题吗？ “客户可以选择他们是否想留下并在应用程序上购买或做出其他方式，对吧？” 
埃文斯承认，尼西反向指导规定“不会消除苹果在这里的市场力量，但它肯定会削弱它。”他说这对一些应用程序比其他应用程序更有用 - 对于拥有独立网站的基于订阅的公司来说，这对依赖于微调流依赖的移动游戏来说非常有用。但他承认他没有进行对这个话题的具体研究，所以他不确定问题仍然有多大。
  在当天晚些时候，经济学家Susan Athey将App Store独家级别提出了一个不同的问题。 App Store允许用户注册订阅，但如果它们切换到Android手机，他们必须取消他们的订阅或通过Apple继续管理。 Athey正在使用它来解释为什么一个第三方App Store会有用，如果苹果曾经允许一个存在 - 如果您可以访问两个大电话平台的同一购买，同样可以获得您的旧iOS应用程序新的iPhone，交换设备可能变得更加容易。
 但罗杰斯再次建议，如果开发人员可以告诉人们通过网络注册，“那么真的是对你所谈论的那种跨平台应用商店的同样的需求。”毕竟，Netflix这样的服务已经直接通过他们的网站注册 -  Apple和Google真的不喜欢它，并且他们试图在没有实际禁令的情况下劝阻练习。与埃文斯类似，艾森承认，让应用程序制造商“提醒人们获得最有效的支付方式”。
 Athey认为，在尝试完成那种活动时，“消费者确实会得到klutzy并断开连接和敏感，延迟，”告诉人们使用Web浏览器并不能解决这个问题。但罗杰斯可以轻松地决定不便和强制无知是单独的问题，只有后者只是一个严重的反垄断关注。
 摆脱反向指导规定将是史诗的相对小的胜利，希望将全面的第三方应用商店放在iOS上。但它是一个智能手机生态系统特征，经常被更大的反托拉斯投诉 - 和Epic v。苹果在聚光灯下放置。
note_en:
Last week, the judge in  Epic v. Apple asked whether Epic really had an antitrust case against Apple, or whether it just wanted to  help kids make impulse purchases. Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers was talking about the importance of where and how  people pay for their apps, and today she continued that line of questioning to the point of suggesting a kind of App Store policy change that Epic never originally put on the table.
 Epic sued Apple for banning  Fortnite from iOS over a direct payment system for V-Bucks,  Fortnite’s in-game currency. Epic called that unfair and monopolistic. But Apple argued that it lets developers sell in-app purchases through its Safari browser, even at a discounted price — so there’s no lockout. And while Epic itself has focused on explaining  why web apps aren’t a good substitute for native ones, its expert witness David Evans brought up another major issue: anti-steering rules.
 Anti-steering rules (in this context)  refer to rules that ban developers from pointing users outside of Apple’s ecosystem. iOS developers can’t add links or references telling people to get a better deal on their website, or send emails to accounts created through Apple. Android has these rules too, but it  delayed a serious crackdown on them until this fall — and since you can install third-party stores and sideload apps on Android, developers in Google’s ecosystem have more options in general.
  Evans, an economist, was originally trying to explain in-app purchases by comparing Apple to a ride-hailing app like Uber, comparing an app developer to a Uber driver who had struck up a good relationship with a customer. The customer wanted to start directly hiring the driver, but the ride-hailing company (representing Apple) demanded that the customer keep paying through its app.
 Judge Rogers didn’t appear convinced. Buying V-Bucks through a browser, she noted, seemed a lot like a passenger directly paying a driver. “There’s nothing about that distribution process that impacted differently given your Uber example.”
 Evans basically responded that in this analogy, cab drivers can’t even do the equivalent of giving passengers their phone numbers. “Epic is not able to message the iOS app user and tell them ‘You can go to the web and get this more cheaply.’ Or ‘I really encourage you to go to the web and get V-Bucks there,’” Evans objected. The problem, he said, was the combination of requiring Epic to use Apple payment processing, plus a “whole set of barriers” that make it harder to tell users they have an alternative.
 These anti-steering provisions have come up in the trial before — yoga app maker Yoga Buddhi complained about them last week. But this time, Rogers offered an obvious followup question. If there was no anti-steering provision, she asked, would Epic still have a problem with Apple’s system? “The customer could choose whether they wanted to stay and make the purchase on the app or do it some other way, right?”
 Evans admitted that nixing the anti-steering provisions “wouldn’t eliminate the market power that Apple has here, but it would certainly diminish it.” He said it would be more helpful for some apps than others — it’s pretty good for subscription-based companies that have a separate website, for instance, and less useful for mobile-only games that rely on a stream of microtransactions. But he acknowledged he hadn’t conducted a specific study of the topic, so he wasn’t sure exactly  how big the issue would still be.
  Later in the day, economist Susan Athey raised a different issue with App Store exclusivity. The App Store lets users sign up for subscriptions, but if they switch to an Android phone, they have to either cancel their subscription or keep managing it through Apple. Athey was using this to explain why a third-party app store would be useful, should Apple ever allow one to exist — if you could access the same purchase from both big phone platforms, the same way you can get your old iOS apps on a new iPhone, switching devices could become much easier.
 But Rogers suggested again that if developers could just tell people to sign up through the web, “then there wouldn’t really be the same kind of need for the kind of cross-platform app store that you’re talking about.” After all, services like Netflix already direct people to sign up through their websites — Apple and Google just really don’t like it, and they try to  discourage the practice without an actual ban. Similar to Evans, Athey conceded that there’d be a “big benefit” in letting app makers “alert people to the most efficient way to pay.”
 Athey argued that “consumers do get klutzy and disconnected and sensitive to delays when trying to complete that type of activity,” and telling people to go use a web browser doesn’t solve that problem. But Rogers could easily decide that inconvenience and enforced ignorance are separate issues, and that only the latter is a serious antitrust concern.
 Getting rid of anti-steering provisions would be a comparatively small win for Epic, which wants to put full-fledged third-party App Stores on iOS. But it’s a smartphone ecosystem feature that’s often overshadowed by bigger antitrust complaints — and  Epic v. Apple is putting it under the spotlight.
title: EPIC v。苹果在苹果的反转向规则上闪耀着光线，这阻止开发人员指向用户更好地在他们的网站或其他地方交易
title_en: Epic v. Apple is shining a light on Apple's anti-steering rules, which prevent developers from pointing users to better deals on their website or elsewhere

note_en:
A Facebook moderator has for the first time given evidence revealing the mental toll of the job, to a parliamentary committee.
 The Irish parliament heard how moderators viewed graphic content up to eight hours a day.
 Law firm Foxglove and the Communication Workers Union, representing moderators, called for better psychological support and freedom to speak out.
  Isabella Plunkett has worked as a Facebook content moderator for just over two years, and still works there.
 Her job is to review posts on the platform - which can contain graphic violence, exploitation, extremism, abuse and suicide.
 The 26-year-old says she could not speak to her friends or family about the things she saw at work due to a non-disclosure agreement (NDA) which she had signed at the beginning of her contract.
 Members of Ireland's Joint Committee on Enterprise, Trade and Employment, commended her bravery in speaking out.
   “I'm here speaking out and I don't actually necessarily know in detail what I'm legally allowed to say and not to say,” she said.
 “It was always clear we couldn't speak about our job, we couldn't speak about our job to friends, family... and it's definitely a workplace with a sense of secrecy.”
 Facebook told the BBC that NDAs are standard practice and that reviewers can discuss any aspect of their job with doctors and counsellors.
 Staff can discuss the general challenges and rewards of their jobs with family and loved ones, but not specific details of the content they are reviewing.
  “I’ve done the job for two years and I don’t think I could do it for much longer because of the strain it does cause to your mental health,” Isabella told the BBC.
 “It's not like a normal job where you can go to work and go home and forget about it - the stuff you’re seeing is really ingrained in your mind.’”
 Isabella processes around 100 "tickets" a day - these can be videos, images or text posts on the platform. She said they often contain graphic violence, suicide, exploitation and abuse.
  Isabella claims she was not allowed to work from home, unlike her counterparts who were employed directly by Facebook who did the same job.
 As a result, she says she is exposed to more graphic content, because she is in the office.
  “The high priority queues - the graphic violence, the child stuff, the exploitation and the suicides, people working from home don’t get that - the burden is put on us.”
 Despite having family shielding at home, she was told to come into the office and developed anxiety, for which she now takes antidepressants.
 “Every day was a nightmare,” she said, adding that the support given was “insufficient.”
 Facebook says psychological help is available to all its moderators 24 hours a day, but Isabella claims its wellness coaches are not qualified psychiatrists.
 “I was seeing the wellness team but didn’t feel I got the support I needed. I can’t say I left work feeling relieved or knowing I could go home and have a good night's sleep - that’s not possible,” she added.
 “It would follow me home. I could just be watching TV at home and think back to one of the horrible, really graphic tickets.”
  Sub-contracted staff are given 1.5 hours of "wellness" time a week, she says, which can be used for speaking to a wellness coach, going for walks or taking time out when feeling overwhelmed.
 “It’s not enough. I’m now seeing the content I view in work in my dreams. I remember it, I experience it again and it is horrible.
 “You never know what is going to come next and you have to watch it the full way through because they might have violators.”
  Isabella says she was also asked to sign a disclaimer before starting work, accepting that the content seen in her job could lead to poor mental health and PTSD (Post Traumatic Stress Disorder).
 An example of the contract, read out in the committee said: “I understand that exposure to this content may give me post traumatic stress disorder.
 "I will engage in a mandatory wellness coaching session but I understand that those are not conditions and may not be sufficient to prevent my contracting PTSD.”
 A Facebook spokeswoman said: "Everyone who reviews content for Facebook goes through an in-depth training programme on Facebook's Community Standards and has access to psychological support to ensure their wellbeing.
 “We are committed to working with our partners to provide support for our content reviewers as we recognise that reviewing certain types of content can sometimes be hard," she added.
 "In Ireland, this includes 24/7 on-site support with trained practitioners, an on-call service, and access to private healthcare from the first day of employment.
 "We are also employing technical solutions to limit their exposure to potentially graphic material as much as possible. This is an important issue, and we are committed to getting this right.”
    But Isabella said this was a Facebook "fantasy", that systems were “not even near that stage”.
 Speaking to the committee, Isabella said “people are intimidated” by the NDA process and afraid of losing their jobs.
 She cited an internal communications platform on Facebook, in which workers' posts were deleted when speaking up. Facebook denied these claims and said no disciplinary action is taken for employees raising concerns.
 “People complained about the treatment and what was going on and how they felt unsafe,” Isabella told the committee. “It was clear that it was being censored because people's comments were being deleted, accounts were being disabled."
 She said her experience drove her to give evidence: “I just had such a feeling that I needed to do it,” she added in her testimony. “I need to speak for the people that are too afraid, that feel they have too many responsibilities, and they can't afford to take any risks."
title: Facebook主持人：'每天都是噩梦'
title_en: Facebook moderator: ‘Every day was a nightmare’

note_en:
The Consumer Price Index rise for April from a year earlier was the sharpest since September 2008.
  One big reason for the acceleration was base effects – at this time a year ago, the economy was hit with the worst of the Covid pandemic and inflation was unusually low.
 Federal Reserve officials see the current rise as temporary and not likely to influence policy.
 Inflation in April accelerated at its fastest pace in more than 12 years as the U.S. economic recovery kicked into gear and energy prices jumped higher, the Labor Department reported Wednesday.
 The Consumer Price Index, which measures a basket of goods as well as energy and housing costs, rose 4.2% from a year earlier. A Dow Jones survey had expected a 3.6% increase. The month-to-month gain was 0.8%, against the expected 0.2%.
  Excluding volatile food and energy prices, the core CPI increased 3% from the same period in 2020 and 0.9% on a monthly basis. The respective estimates were 2.3% and 0.3%.
  Energy prices overall jumped 25% from a year earlier, including a 49.6% increase for gasoline and 37.3% for fuel oil. That came even though most energy categories saw a decline in April.
 Prices at the pump, which fell 1.4% in April, have resumed their climb in May, with the national average eclipsing $3 a gallon for the first time since November 2014, according to AAA. Further rises are likely from Friday's cyberattack that shut down Colonial Pipeline's main transmission line from Houston to New Jersey.
 Used car and truck prices, which are seen as a key inflation indicator, surged 21%, including a 10% increase in April alone. Shelter, another key CPI component, was up 2.1% year over year and 0.4% for the month.
  In addition to rising prices, one of the main reasons for the big annual gain was because of base effects, meaning inflation was very low at this time in 2020 as the Covid pandemic caused a widespread shutdown of the U.S. economy. Year-over-year comparisons are going to be distorted for a few months because of the pandemic's impact.
 For that reason, Federal Reserve policymakers and many economists  are dismissing the current round of numbers as transitory, with the expectation that inflation settles down later this year around the 2% range targeted by the central bank.
 Fed officials repeatedly have said they will not raise interest rates  or pull back on monthly bond purchases until inflation averages around 2% over an extended period.
 "As the cyclically-sensitive components of CPI are still rising at a modest pace, we doubt this report will change the view of officials that inflationary pressures are 'largely transitory,'" wrote Michael Pearce, senior U.S. economist at Capital Economics. "It's just that there's a lot more 'transitory' than they were expecting."
 Stock market futures  briefly reached session lows after the release of the CPI numbers and pointed to a negative open on Wall Street. Government bond yields were mostly higher.
 Price surges also have come amid supply bottlenecks caused by a number of factors, from production issues with the ubiquitous semiconductors found in electronics products to the Suez Canal blockage in March to soaring demand for a variety of commodities.
 Lumber prices alone have risen 124% in 2021 amid persistent demand for building materials. Copper, often seen as a proxy for economic activity, has jumped nearly 36%.

多年来，在线安全法案一直在作品中 - 在此期间，在此期间需要年龄计划在英国访问在线色情的时间，而且还具有保护孩子免受暴露在网上的不适当内容，但被广泛批评为不可行，悄然下降。
 当政府表示，它将侧重于介绍全面立法来规范一系列在线危害。它现在可以说这是这样做的。
  145页的在线安全帐单可以在Gov.uk网站上找到 - 以及123页的解释性说明和146页的影响评估。
 立法草案对数字服务提供商的关注责任以防止用户在线接触非法和/或有害物质的方式进行中等用户生成的内容。
 政府将该计划推出全球“开创性”和索赔将迎来“技术的新责任时代，为在线世界带来公平和问责制”。
 批评者警告提案将通过鼓励向过度审查员促进平台损害表达自由，同时为将劝阻技术创新的数字企业创造了主要的法律和运营头痛。
  该法案将由国会议员联合委员会审查 - 在最终版本正式介绍于今年晚些时候辩论的议会之前。 
达到法规书可能需要多长时间并不清楚，但政府在议会中有很大的大多数，所以在其自身的行列中失败了主要的公共哗然和/或大规模反对，在线安全法案有一个明确的道路成为法律的道路。
 在评论声明中，Digital秘书Oliver Dowden表示：“今天英国显示全球领导地位，与我们的开创性法律迎来了新的技术问责制，为在线世界带来公平和问责制。
 “我们将保护儿童在互联网上，打击社交媒体的种族主义滥用，并通过新措施保护自由，创造一个真正民主的数字时代。”
 政府起草在线安全账单的时间长度强调了试图“规范互联网”所涉及的立法挑战。
 在一点弗洛伊德单据中，DCMS的PRO讨论了“政府斗争使互联网安全”。当然，肯定是谁是英国在线安全法律的未来赢家和输家。
  在关于该计划的新闻稿中，数字，媒体，文化和体育部门（DCMS）声称“民主法律”将“让孩子安全，停止种族仇恨和保护民主”。
 但是，由于那些抓住的标题目标意味着这里有一个可怕的地段 - 如果最终结果是一个不连贯的矛盾规则，那么事情就会出错的巨大潜力，这使得数字企业更加难以运作和互联网用户访问所需的内容。 
该法律设定为广泛应用 - 而不仅仅适用于科技巨头或社交媒体网站，而是广泛的托管用户生成内容的网站，应用程序和服务，或者只是允许人们在线与他人交谈。
 在范围服务中，将面临法律要求，以删除和/或限制非法和（在较大的服务的情况下）有害内容的蔓延，这与在这种新的护理义务中失败的严重处罚的风险。还将有要求向执法人员报告儿童性剥削内容。
 OFCOM，英国的Comms Regulator  - 负责规范广播媒体和电信部门 - 在计划下也被设定为成为英国互联网的内容看门狗。
 通过将罚款高达1800万英镑或10％的全球营业额（以较高者为准），将其拨入用户失败的制裁公司对用户的罚款失败的制裁公司。
 监管机构还将阻止访问网站的权力 - 因此禁止审查整个平台的可能性。
 一些竞选人员支持艰难的新互联网规则一直迫使政府包括刑事制裁威胁，以便将C-Suite思想集中在反危害的遵守情况下。虽然部长们没有走得走得走远，但DCMS表示，对于高级管理人员而言，已被列为延期的权力 - 补充说：“如果科技公司没有加强努力，可以在以后推出这一点安全。”
 尽管英国有普遍的公众支持互联网平台规则，但魔鬼是您建议的详细信息。 
民权活动人员和技术政策专家检讨了政府的计划风险，通过强迫私营公司成为言论警察对在线表达进行寒冷的影响。
 法律专家也在警告框架将如何合动，难以定义“危害”等概念 - 以及以新的补充，这些内容被定义为“民主的重要”（政府希望某些平台有特别义务保护）。
 明显的风险是巨大的法律不确定性包装数字企业 - 突出对英国初创企业创新和服务的可用性的影响。
 该法案早期的化身 -  2019年白皮书 - 在标题中有“危害”这个词。已经交换了更多的anodyne参考“安全”，但法律不确定性尚未被交出。
 重点仍然是试图遏制“危害”的无定形集团 - 一些非法，其他人只是令人不愉快 - 这与在线活动有各种各样的联系或与在线活动相关联。 （通常在高调媒体报告的后面，例如在Instagram等平台上对儿童接触自杀内容。）
  这可以从欺凌和滥用（在线拖击），以非法内容（儿童性剥削）的传播，到内容仅对儿童看（法律色情）来说。
 某些类型的在线诈骗（浪漫欺诈）是政府每种危害，政府希望每次出现立法来解决问题。 
伞“危害”框架使英国的方法与欧盟的数字服务法案不同 - 一个平行的立法建议，以更新欧盟的数字规则，更紧密地专注于非法的事物，并将集团制定规则以规范报告程序非法内容;并打击在电子商务市场上销售的危险产品的风险，并“了解您的客户要求”。
  在对英国法案对在线表达的潜在影响的批评的反应中，政府增加了今天所说的措施，旨在加强人们在线自由表达自己的权利。
 它还表示，它在新闻的保障措施中增加，并在英国保护民主政治辩论。
 然而，它的方法已经提高了问题 - 包括一些看起来像一些非常矛盾的规定。
 例如，DCMS对票据如何处理新闻内容的讨论证实，新闻出版商自己的网站上的内容不会在法律范围内（对这些网站的读者评论也不在范围内）和“公认的新闻出版商“在范围内（如社交媒体网站）分享，将免于可能适用于非新闻内容的法律要求。
 实际上，平台将有法律要求保护新闻内容的访问。 （“这意味着[数字平台]必须考虑新闻在进行内容审核时的重要性，有一个快速的上诉过程，用于记者删除内容，并将通过截止日常清除新闻内容， “DCMS笔记。）
 然而，政府还规定，“公民记者的内容将具有与专业记者的内容相同的保护” - 这正是在“公认的”新闻发布商（范围内），公民记者（也是超出范围），只有任何旧的人博客或互联网上的东西（范围......也许？）都会用于引人注目的观点。 
保护政治演讲的雕刻也使数字服务的内容适度图片复杂化，例如，举办种族主义意见的极端主义团体如何寻求憎恨言论并滥用“政治观点”。 （一些彩票的种族主义活动家也喜欢声称是“记者”......）
 DCMS将“禁止歧视特定的政治观点禁止，无论是其关联，无论其相关联，如何将保护保护同样适用于一系列政治意见。
 “保护此类内容的政策需要清楚地列出，可访问的条款和条件，并且公司需要坚持从他们或面对的行动，”它继续下去，补充说：“当抚养内容时，公司需要考虑到为什么内容正在共享的政治背景，如果它是民主的重要性，那么将其提供高度保护。“
 平台将面临平衡所有这些相互矛盾的要求的责任 - 遵守尊重言论自由的实践守则，尊重欧洲州的言论自由 - 但在奥姆彻底遭到挑选的主要处罚威胁，如果他们弄错了。
 有趣的是，政府似乎在Facebook制定的“监督委员会的模型”上看起来有利，其中一个人类人类坐在判断“复杂”内容适度案件中 - 也劝阻太多使用AI过滤器，它警告风险失踪语音细胞和过度删除内容。 （特别是鉴于英国政府对平台的最终压力采用AI工具来加速恐怖主义内容抛弃时，特别有趣。）
 “该法案将确保英国人民可以在线自由在线表达并参与多元化和强大的辩论，”DCMS写道。 “所有占地面议的公司都需要考虑并在履行职责时对履约自由进行保障。这些保障措施将在惯例中阐述，但是，例如，可能包括人类主持人在上下文重要的复杂案例中采取决定。“
 “使用他们的服务的人需要获得有效的上诉航线，因为没有充分的理由，公司必须恢复该内容，如果它被不公平地删除。用户还将呼吁奥福姆，这些投诉将成为OFCOM的地平线扫描，研究和执法活动的重要组成部分，“它继续。 
“第1类服务[最大，最受欢迎的服务]将有额外的职责。他们需要对其对表达自由的影响，并展示他们已经采取了措施减轻任何不利影响的最新评估。这些措施消除了在线公司采取限制性措施或过度删除内容，以满足其新的在线安全职责的风险。这个例子可以是AI适度技术虚假地将无害的内容标记为有害的，例如讽刺。“
 该计划的另一个令人困惑的组成部分是，虽然条例草案包括解决它所谓的“用户生成欺诈”的措施 - 例如在社交媒体上的帖子进行假冒投资机会或浪漫诈骗在约会应用程序 - 通过广告在线进行的欺诈行为，电子邮件或克隆的网站不会在每个DCMS的范围内，因为它说“该法案侧重于通过用户生成内容犯下的危害”。
 然而，由于互联网用户可以轻松和廉价地创建和运行在线广告 - 因为Facebook这样的平台基本上向任何愿意付钱的人提供了广告目标工具 - 那么为什么要欺骗广告作为豁免欺诈？
 绘制线条似乎是一个毫无意义的地方。欺诈，有人支付了几美元以放大他们的骗局似乎对欺诈似乎不如免费的Facebook帖子，这些欺诈似乎是链接到自同样加密投资骗局的免费Facebook。
 简而言之，通过区分创造不连贯和令人困惑的规则，存在任意/不安的危险的风险。这对任何人的在线安全都不好。
 与此同时，政府正在制定一项雄心勃勃的专业竞争政权，专门调节科技巨头。确保平台巨头框架与这些更广泛的数字伤害规则之间的相干性和避免相互冲突或重叠的要求是进一步的挑战。
note_en:
The Online Safety Bill has been in the works for years — during which time a prior plan to require age verification for accessing online porn in the UK, also with the goal of protecting kids from being exposed to inappropriate content online but which was widely criticized as unworkable, got  quietly dropped.
 At the time the government said it would focus on introducing comprehensive legislation to regulate a range of online harms. It can now say it’s done that.
  The 145-page Online Safety Bill can be  found here on the gov.uk website — along with 123 pages of explanatory notes and an 146-page impact assessment.
 The draft legislation imposes a duty of care on digital service providers to moderate user generated content in a way that prevents users from being exposed to illegal and/or harmful stuff online.
 The government dubs the plan globally “groundbreaking” and claims it will usher in “a new age of accountability for tech and bring fairness and accountability to the online world”.
 Critics warn the proposals will harm freedom of expression by encouraging platforms to over-censor, while also creating major legal and operational headaches for digital businesses that will discourage tech innovation.
  The bill will be scrutinised by a joint committee of MPs — before a final version is formally introduced to Parliament for debate later this year.
 How long it might take to hit the statute books isn’t clear but the government has a large majority in parliament so, failing major public uproar and/or mass opposition within its own ranks, the Online Safety Bill has a clear road to becoming law.
 Commenting in a statement, digital secretary Oliver Dowden said: “Today the UK shows global leadership with our groundbreaking laws to usher in a new age of accountability for tech and bring fairness and accountability to the online world.
 “We will protect children on the internet, crack down on racist abuse on social media and through new measures to safeguard our liberties, create a truly democratic digital age.”
 The length of time it’s taken for the government to draft the  Online Safety Bill underscores the legislative challenge involved in trying to ‘regulate the Internet’.
 In a bit of a Freudian slip, the DCMS’ own PR talks about “the government’s fight to make the internet safe”. And there are certainly question-marks over who the future winners and losers of the UK’s Online Safety laws will be.
  In a  press release about the plan, the Department for Digital, Media, Culture and Sport (DCMS) claimed the “landmark laws” will “keep children safe, stop racial hate and protect democracy online”.
 But as that grab-bag of headline goals implies there’s an awful lot going on here — and huge potential for things to go wrong if the end result is an incoherent mess of contradictory rules that make it harder for digital businesses to operate and for Internet users to access the content they need.
 The laws are set to apply widely — not just to tech giants or social media sites but to a broad swathe of websites, apps and services that host user-generated content or just allow people to talk to others online.
 In scope services will face a legal requirement to remove and/or limit the spread of illegal and (in the case of larger services) harmful content, with the risk of major penalties for failing in this new duty of care toward users. There will also be requirements for reporting child sexual exploitation content to law enforcement.
 Ofcom, the UK’s comms regulator — which is responsible for regulating the broadcast media and telecoms sectors — is set to become the  UK Internet’s content watchdog too, under the plan.
 It will have powers to sanction companies that fail in the new duty of care toward users by hitting them with fines of up to £18M or ten per cent of annual global turnover (whichever is higher).
 The regulator will also get the power to block access to sites — so the potential for censoring entire platforms is baked in.
 Some campaigners backing tough new Internet rules have been pressing the government to include the threat of criminal sanctions for CEOs to concentrate C-suite minds on anti-harms compliance. And while ministers haven’t gone that far, DCMS says a new criminal offence for senior managers has been included as a deferred power — adding: “This could be introduced at a later date if tech firms don’t step up their efforts to improve safety.”
 Despite there being widespread public support in the UK for tougher rules for Internet platforms, the devil  is the detail of how exactly you propose to do that.
 Civil rights campaigners and tech policy experts have warned from the get-go that the government’s plan risks having a chilling effect on online expression by forcing private companies to be speech police.
 Legal experts are also warning over how workable the framework will be, given hard to define concepts like “harms” — and, in a new addition, content that’s defined as “democratically important” (which the government wants certain platforms to have a special duty to protect).
 The clear risk is massive legal uncertainty wrapping digital businesses — with knock-on impacts on startup innovation and availability of services in the UK.
 The bill’s earlier incarnation — a  2019 White Paper  — had the word “harms” in the title. That’s been swapped for a more anodyne reference to “safety” but the legal uncertainty hasn’t been swapped out.
 The emphasis remains on trying to rein in an amorphous conglomerate of ‘harms’ — some illegal, others just unpleasant — that have been variously linked to or associated with online activity. (Often off the back of high profile media reporting, such as into  children’s exposure to suicide content on platforms like Instagram.)
  This can range from bullying and abuse (online trolling), to the spread of illegal content (child sexual exploitation), to content that’s merely inappropriate for children to see (legal pornography).
 Certain types of online scams (romance fraud) are another harm the government wants the legislation to address, per latest additions.
 The umbrella ‘harms’ framing makes the UK approach distinct to the  European Union’s Digital Service Act — a parallel legislative proposal to update the EU’s digital rules that’s more tightly focused on things that are illegal, with the bloc setting out rules to standardize reporting procedures for illegal content; and combating the risk of dangerous products being sold on ecommerce marketplaces with ‘know your customer’ requirements.
  In a response to criticism of the UK Bill’s potential impact on online expression, the government has added measures which it said today are aimed at strengthen people’s rights to express themselves freely online.
 It also says it’s added in safeguards for journalism and to protect democratic political debate in the UK.
 However its approach is already raising questions — including over what look like some pretty contradictory stipulations.
 For example, the DCMS’ discussion of how the bill will handle journalistic content confirms that content on news publishers’ own websites won’t be in scope of the law (reader comments on those sites are also not in scope) and that articles by “recognised news publishers” shared on in-scope services (such as social media sites) will be exempted from legal requirements that may otherwise apply to non journalistic content.
 Indeed, platforms will have a legal requirement to safeguard access to journalism content. (“This means [digital platforms] will have to consider the importance of journalism when undertaking content moderation, have a fast-track appeals process for journalists’ removed content, and will be held to account by Ofcom for the arbitrary removal of journalistic content,” DCMS notes.)
 However the government also specifies that “citizen journalists’ content will have the same protections as professional journalists’ content” — so exactly where (or how) the line gets drawn between “recognized” news publishers (out of scope), citizen journalists (also out of scope), and just any old person blogging or posting stuff on the Internet (in scope… maybe?) is going to make for compelling viewing.
 Carve outs to protect political speech also complicate the content moderation picture for digital services — given, for example, how extremist groups that hold racist opinions can seek to launder their hate speech and abuse as ‘political opinion’. (Some notoriously racist activists also like to claim to be ‘journalists’…)
 DCMS writes that companies will be “forbidden from discriminating against particular political viewpoints and will need to apply protections equally to a range of political opinions, no matter their affiliation”.
 “Policies to protect such content will need to be set out in clear and accessible terms and conditions and firms will need to stick to them or face enforcement action from Ofcom,” it goes on, adding: “When moderating content, companies will need to take into account the political context around why the content is being shared and give it a high level of protection if it is democratically important.”
 Platforms will face responsibility for balancing all these conflicting requirements — drawing on Codes of Practice on content moderation that respects freedom of expression which will be set out by Ofcom — but also under threat of major penalties being slapped on them by Ofcom if they get it wrong.
 Interestingly, the government appears to be looking favorably on the  Facebook-devised ‘Oversight Board’ model, where a panel of humans sit in judgement on ‘complex’ content moderation cases — and also discouraging too much use of AI filters which it warns risk missing speech nuance and over-removing content. (Especially interesting given the  UK government’s prior pressure on platforms to adopt AI tools to speed up terrorism content takedowns.)
 “The Bill will ensure people in the UK can express themselves freely online and participate in pluralistic and robust debate,” writes DCMS. “All in-scope companies will need to consider and put in place safeguards for freedom of expression when fulfilling their duties. These safeguards will be set out by Ofcom in codes of practice but, for example, might include having human moderators take decisions in complex cases where context is important.”
 “People using their services will need to have access to effective routes of appeal for content removed without good reason and companies must reinstate that content if it has been removed unfairly. Users will also be able to appeal to Ofcom and these complaints will form an essential part of Ofcom’s horizon-scanning, research and enforcement activity,” it goes on.
 “Category 1 services [the largest, most popular services] will have additional duties. They will need to conduct and publish up-to-date assessments of their impact on freedom of expression and demonstrate they have taken steps to mitigate any adverse effects. These measures remove the risk that online companies adopt restrictive measures or over-remove content in their efforts to meet their new online safety duties. An example of this could be AI moderation technologies falsely flagging innocuous content as harmful, such as satire.”
 Another confusing-looking component of the plan is that while the bill includes measures to tackle what it calls “user-generated fraud” — such as posts on social media for fake investment opportunities or romance scams on dating apps — fraud that’s conducted online via advertising, emails or cloned websites will not be in scope, per DCMS, as it says “the Bill focuses on harm committed through user-generated content”.
 Yet since Internet users can easily and cheaply create and run online ads — as platforms like Facebook essentially offer their ad targeting tools to anyone who’s willing to pay — then why carve out fraud by ads as exempt?
 It seems a meaningless place to draw the line. Fraud where someone paid a few dollars to amplify their scam doesn’t seem a less harmful class of fraud than a free Facebook post linking to the self-same crypto investment scam.
 In short, there’s a risk of arbitrary/ill-thought through distinctions creating incoherent and confusing rules that are prone to loopholes. Which doesn’t sound good for anyone’s online safety.
 In parallel, meanwhile, the government is devising an ambitious  pro-competition ex ante regime to regulate tech giants specifically. Ensuring coherence and avoiding conflicting or overlapping requirements between that framework for platform giants and these wider digital harms rules is a further challenge.
title: 英国出版在线安全账单草案
title_en: UK publishes draft Online Safety Bill

您好，欢迎回到股权，TechCrunch的风险资本聚焦播客，在那里我们解开了头条新闻背后的数字。
 对于本周的深度潜水，亚历克斯和丹尼未包装的Natasha的最新项目：Duolingo EC-1。上周发表了12,000个单词四部分系列，值得一。但是，直到您到达它，享受我们的播客，使双击键入最有趣的位。
  在我们让我们的早晨过敏队的方式之后，这是怎么回事：
 什么是EC-1？ TechCrunch风格深入进入初创公司最有前途和有趣的公司之一。
 什么是上面的飞行虫子？那是Duolingo的吉祥物。这是一种高速和谦虚的威胁的组合。 （你会很开心学习语言。或者猫头鹰将访问。）
 为什么我们写关于Duolingo？不，这不仅是因为Duolingo是Edtech。娜塔莎挖掘了公司的产品LED增长模式，并对游戏化的看法，令人着迷。
 今天的展示名称怎么了？事实证明，Duolingo有一个火种角度。事实上，Duolingo在设计和货币化时倾向于其中一些最大的公司。 
Duolingo EC-1包含四个主要物品，编号为12,200字和48分钟的阅读时间。这是商店里的内容：
 第1部分：原点故事“如何将机器人战斗测试变成了Edtech最具标志性的品牌，Duolingo”（3,300字/ 13分钟） - 看看危地马拉移民企业家Luis von Ahn如何枢转，在登录屏幕上与Squiggly Text进行争取建立埃德泰克的伟大成功案例之一。
 第2部分：产品主导的增长战略“Edtech最下载的应用程序的产品LED增长”（3,000字/ 12分钟） - 分析了EDTECH公司必须评估的策略和权衡，因为它从数千到5亿注册学习者。
 第3部分：货币化“Duolingo如何流利地发挥货币化”（2,800字/ 11分钟） - 检查Duolingo如何用各种不同的商业模式进行实验，以匹配其独特的社区，以及它最终选择订阅的原因。
 第4部分：新举措和未来的前景“Duolingo不能教你如何讲一种语言，但现在它想尝试”（3,100字/ 12分钟） - 探索Duolingo如何推出新的业务线，他们的成功机会是如何启动新的业务线，以及如何试图将其主要产品从基本的语言流畅扩展到掌握，同时为混合添加口语技能。
 当然，使用代码额外的紧缩“股票”为甜蜜，也许是最好的，折扣访问这个故事和所有最好的东西。
  股权每周一下午7点，星期三，星期三和星期五早上在上午7:00。PST，所以在苹果播客，阴天，Spotify和所有演员中订阅我们。
note_en:
Hello and welcome back to  Equity, TechCrunch’s venture capital-focused podcast, where we unpack the numbers behind the headlines.
 For this week’s deep dive,  Alex and  Danny unpacked  Natasha‘s latest project:  The Duolingo EC-1. The 12,000 word four-part series was published last week and is worth a read. But, until you get to it, enjoy our podcast that doubles-clicks into its most interesting bits.
  Here’s how it went, after we got our morning allergy banter out of the way:
 What’s an EC-1? A TechCrunch-style deep-dive into one of the startup world’s most promising, and interesting companies.
 What’s with the flying vermin up above? That’s Duolingo’s mascot. Which is a combination of hypercutness and modest menace. (You  will have fun learning a language. Or the owl  will visit.)
 Why did we write about Duolingo? No, it wasn’t only because Duolingo is edtech. Natasha dug into  the company’s product-led growth mode, and its views on gamification, which were fascinating.
 What’s up with today’s show name? As it turns out, Duolingo has a  Tinder angle. In fact, Duolingo leaned on some of the biggest companies out there when it came to design and monetization.
 The Duolingo EC-1 comprises four main articles numbering 12,200 words and a reading time of 48 minutes. Here’s what’s in store:
 Part 1: Origin story “ How a bot-fighting test turned into edtech’s most iconic brand, Duolingo” (3,300 words/13 minutes) — looks at how Guatemalan immigrant entrepreneur Luis von Ahn pivoted from fighting bot attacks on login screens with squiggly text to building one of edtech’s great success stories.
 Part 2: Product-led growth strategy “ The product-led growth behind edtech’s most downloaded app” (3,000 words/12 minutes) — analyzes the tactics and tradeoffs that an edtech company has to evaluate as it grows from thousands to 500 million registered learners.
 Part 3: Monetization “ How Duolingo became fluent in monetization” (2,800 words/11 minutes) — examines how Duolingo experimented with a variety of different business models to match its unique community, and why it chose subscription in the end.
 Part 4: New initiatives and future outlook “ Duolingo can’t teach you how to speak a language, but now it wants to try” (3,100 words/12 minutes) — explores how Duolingo is launching new business lines, their chances for success, and how the company is attempting to expand its main product from basic language fluency to mastery while adding speaking skills to the mix.

该公司于2015年推出，目的是简化发票管理进程。那为什么需要？服务和软件由公司跨越各个部门的员工购买。由此产生的发票然后降落在金融部门，这是一部分的公司，可以有点淤泥。然后留下金融来确定许多因素，就像为什么购买的原因，无论是交付的，还有发票是否应该支付。
 Stampli将每个账单转变为通信集线器，将金融的人与其他利益相关者联系在一起，包括供应商。它的系统使用机器学习识别组织如何分配成本，管理批准工作流程以及可以从发票中提取的数据来识别模式。
  Stampli一直是历史上付款平台不可知论，这意味着其客户可以使用他们想要的任何付款方式或提供者。然而，在2020年代初期，Stampli推出了直接支付，其自身支付产品可以解决会计部门的一些贴装问题。这适合看到现代软件公司将付款选项纳入其服务的通用趋势，在此过程中为他们的书籍增加了收入流。
  由于Stampli的核心产品专注于个性交易的背景，因此它与其付款产品展现得很好。例如，通过ACH（以低价为低价）支付的人通常会遭受支付方法喜欢将多个事务分组成单个和删除上下文的事实。该问题可以使其巨大的头痛，以便应付以协调个人购买。但由于Stampli已经了解各个交易的背景，因此它可以填写会计的空白，同时仍然允许它们使用ACH进行付款。
 当然，Stampli仍然是不可知的，让组织使用他们喜欢的任何提供商。不过，联合创始人和首席执行官eyalfeldman表示，在20％到25％之间的斯坦普利的客户使用直接支付。
 “所有这些支付提供商都可以自我货币化，”布尔德曼说。 “我们向客户提供作为服务的付款，为他们提供管理该部分过程的最简单方法。有一个来自直接支付的收入流，但这不是我们这样做的原因。我们希望将付款转化为商品。“
 Stampli将利用新的资金来调整缩放，旨在在明年增加业务的大小。到目前为止，Stampli拥有大约1000万客户，并管理了200亿美元的交易。
note_en:
The company launched in 2015 with the goal of simplifying the process of invoice management. Why is that needed? Services and software are purchased by employees of companies across a variety of departments. The resulting invoices then land in the finance department, a part of companies that can be a bit siloed. Finance is then left to determine a number of factors, like why something was purchased, whether it delivered, and if the invoice should it be paid at all.
 Stampli turns each bill into a communications hub, connecting the folks in finance to other stakeholders on the purchase, including the vendor. Its system uses machine learning to recognize patterns around how the organization allocates cost, manage approval workflows, and what data can be extracted from invoices.
  Stampli has been historically payment platform agnostic, meaning that its customers could use whatever payment method or provider they wanted. However, in early 2020, Stampli launched Direct Pay, its own payments product that solve some of the stickier problems for the accounting department. This fits into the general trend of seeing modern software companies bake a payments option into their services, adding a revenue stream to their books in the process.
  Because Stampli’s core product focused on the context of individual transactions, it plays well with its payments product. For example, folks who pay through ACH (which comes at a low, flat cost) usually suffer from the fact that the payment method likes to group multiple transactions into a single sum, which removes context. That issue can make it a huge headache for accounts payable to reconcile individual purchases. But because Stampli already understands the context of individual transactions, it can fill in the blanks for accounting while still allowing them to use ACH for payments.
 Of course, Stampli remains payments agnostic, letting organizations use whatever provider they’d like. Still, co-founder and CEO  Eyal Feldman says that between 20 and 25 percent of Stampli’s customers use Direct Pay.
 “There are all these payment providers that monetize on the payments themselves,” said Feldman. “We provide payments as a service to our customers to give them the easiest way to manage that part of the process. There is a revenue stream coming from Direct Pay but that isn’t the reason we do it. We want to turn payments into a commodity.”
 Stampli will use the new funding to sprint into scaling, aiming to double the size of the business in the next year. Thus far, Stampli has about 1000 customers, and has managed $20 billion in transactions.

聊天不仅仅是说明性的，因为我们得知两个CFOS分享他们对延迟公开产品的看法以及当不同类型的首次亮相最有意义时。虽然TechCrunch船员有时是一定的Spac-LED交易，但该对据称交易可以效益。
 谈话的谈话是CakeBread的最近的IPO专注于IPO的书籍，这不仅会议，这些书不仅会宣传公众而不是后来对他们的内部运营有益，而且因为它可以为公众提供有机会参与公司的成功。
 在今天的超负荷的私人市场和泡沫的公共领域，他的论点值得考虑。
 以下是喜欢那些喜欢书面字的人的回顾，但我们还嵌入了下面的整个集中。
   CakeBread说，他开始在首席执行官在首席执行官Mark Benioff工作时塑造他的想法，同时在首席执行官Mark Benioff上仔细考虑是公开的。
 “我们开始意识到的是[那]它让你更高效;它迫使你把系统放在适当的地方;他说，这让你经营业务，而不是让人们赚钱以继续发展业务，“他说。 “从业务角度来看......它总是关于纪律并运行业务并进行。”
 正如Cakebread所检查的那样，他的想法进化了。他指出，在过去十年左右，交易所上市公司的数量下降了50％。这是一个问题。
note_en:
The chat was more than illustrative, as we got to hear two CFOs share their views on delayed public offerings and when different types of debuts can make the most sense. While the TechCrunch crew has, at times, made light of certain SPAC-led deals, the pair argued that the transactions can make good sense.
 Undergirding the conversation was Cakebread’s  recent IPO-focused book, which not only posited that companies going public earlier rather than later is good for their internal operations but also because it can provide the public with a chance to participate in a company’s success.
 In today’s hyper-charged private markets and frothy public domain, his argument is worth considering.
 What follows is the recap for those who prefer the written word, but we’ve also embedded the entire episode below.
   Cakebread said he began shaping his ideas around IPOs while working at Salesforce alongside CEO Mark Benioff while they mulling whether to take the company public.
 “What we started to realize was [that] it makes you more efficient; it forces you to put systems in place; it makes you run a business as opposed to just keep getting money from people to keep growing the business,” he said. “And so from the business angle… it was always about discipline and running a business and getting going.”
 As Cakebread examined other companies and served on boards, his ideas evolved. He noted that the number of listed companies on the exchanges has dropped by 50% over the last decade or so. That’s a problem.

 最重要的是，新的现金注入可以帮助公司终于实现盈利。在踏板车初创公司中，这是一个罕见的高度开销。
 特殊目的采集公司或Spacs已成为公共交通初创公司的流行路线。今年已经是欧洲和美国的Scooter公司Helbiz通过Spac与GreenVision抢购公司的合并，Spac Shell Corporations允许公司列出纳斯达克，而无需进行传统的首次公开发售。
note_en:
Bird said it was able to raise $106 million in private investment in public equity, or PIPE, by institutional investor Fidelity Management & Research Company LLC, and others. Apollo Investment Corp. and MidCap Financial Trust provided an additional $40 million asset financing.
 The transaction will enable the combined entity to retain net proceeds of up to $428 million of cash, according to Switchback, which brings  $316 million cash-in-trust to the table. The announcement also provided new information about a previously undisclosed  $208 million, which Bird raised privately as part of an  April 2021 Senior Preferred Convertible equity offering led by Bracket Capital, Sequoia Capital and Valor Equity Partners.
  When and how Bird would go public has been an item of speculation after  Bloomberg reported last November that the company received “inbound interest” from SPACs.
 Bird’s ride has been bumpy at times. In 2020, revenue dropped to $95 million, or 37% from the previous year. That year the company also laid off around 30% of its workforce – 406 people – for cost-saving reasons.  The company may use this new access to cash to expand its European operations and pay off debt.
 Most importantly, the new injection of cash may help the company finally achieve profitability. It’s a rarity amongst scooter startups, who face notoriously high overhead.
 Special purpose acquisition companies, or SPACs, have become a popular route for going public amongst transportation startups. Already this year,  scooter company Helbiz, which is based in Europe and the U.S., went public via SPAC in a merger with GreenVision Acquisition Corp. SPAC shell corporations allow companies to list on the NASDAQ without doing a traditional initial public offering.

note:
大型时间的工作室本周越来越大，融资了2100万美元的资金，使非良性令牌（NFTS）带到最大的游戏经济体。
 洛杉矶公司首席执行官Arie Meil​​ich首席执行官首席执行官，曾是Decentraland和Game Designer Thor Alexander。
  该资金分为两部分：由FBG资本以及北岛企业，数字货币集团，Okex Blockdream Ventures，Alameda Research，Circor金融和Ashton Kutcher的声音风险投资的1030万美元。大时间工作室也在收取1100万美元的基金的过程中，它将用于投资采用其技术的游戏公司。
 “我们正在创建所有技术，使游戏公司非常容易，”Meilich补充道。 “但作为我们进入市场的方式，我们正在建造第一场比赛，比赛。这是一个多人动作RPG，您可以在那里与最多六个人合作，进入Dungeons，在那里杀死怪物，其中一些将被丢弃贵重的战利品。游戏的设计方式，零区间的人员的经验将开始收集具有真实世界价值的NFT，而无需拥有任何关于区块链技术的知识。最终，我们正试图使技术潜在的技术隐形，并保持用户的价值主张。“
 大时间一室公寓队来包括史诗般的竞赛，防暴游戏，暴雪娱乐，电子艺术和聚光道的校友。他们希望三重游戏公司采用加密货币和NFT技术。但要证明他们的模型工作，他们首先建立自己的动作角色播放游戏，使玩家能够在大多数区块中的障碍中获得基于NFT的虚拟物品。
  现在，NFT是一种热门商品，受到加密货币周围的热潮驱动。 NFT可以独特地将数字物品识别为正宗和稀有的通过区块链技术，透明和安全的数字分类帐。 NFT在其他应用程序中爆炸，例如艺术，体育收藏品和音乐。 NBA顶击（可收集篮球卡上的数字采取）是一个例子。 NBA顶级拍摄的Dapper Labs建造，在公共场合之后五个月超过销售额超过5亿美元。并由艺术家BEEPLE在Christie售出的NFT数字拼贴，以6930万美元。
 作为Defentraland的创始人，Meilich是一个NFT的先驱。在该公司成为一个分散的自治组织（DAO，由买令牌的业主经营），他离开并调查了下一步该做什么。他于2020年4月开始大型工作室。 
“在我们分解项目后，雷神和我开始在这家新公司致力于为大众市场观众划船，”梅希说。 “我们过去几年的学习是，NFTS似乎有很多需求，而是来自一个非常基础的技术观众。缩放到数百万用户 - 我们了解到我们无法通过现有的用户体验来实现，这些用户体验需要建立钱包，让人们定制他们的资产，如果他们错示了他们的私钥，可能会永远失去它们。“
   投资者正在倾倒金钱，但是对于NFTS是一个很好的问题，即NFT是否会成为游戏行业潮湿的小浪潮，或者它们将是一个将扫过它的潮汐。 Meilich希望它将是后者，但他认为必须克服的障碍。北岛福利管理伙伴的特拉维斯Scher在一份声明中表示，在游戏中的市场市场是巨大的，但玩家无权拥有并交易他们购买的物品。他说，在游戏中的NFT可以为玩家提供真正的所有权，并将使得越来越靠近成果的步骤。
 “这是一个为其他游戏公司引入他们经济体的博彩公司的平台，”梅里奇说。 “我们从实现的地方开始，如果你看看Ethereum区块链，还有很多数据显示了人们在NFT上花了多少人。对于一代街区的观众来说，我们看到他们一直在花费比传统的自由游戏所做的比人们更高达六倍。这是一个非常引人注目的商业案例。这对球员来说不仅有益，因为他们有更多的控制物品，但它也应该有利于游戏工作室的经济，因为物品是可转让的。“
 该公司的旗舰标题是跨越空间和时间的冒险。它在校长Albert Einstein经营的时间结束时从学校开始。故事将玩家放入自己的时间机器的驾驶员席位，让他们在亚历山大的采访中收集来自令人惊叹的历史战斗的传奇物品。
 “游戏玩法是从战争之神的快速行动游戏的组合，与魔兽世界和暗黑破坏神一样的合作，有很多程序抢劫系统，”亚历山大说。 “世界本身被分成了三个领域。你有你的魔兽世界，你与其他玩家见面的大空间，并出现了所有酷掠夺者。然后你有这些程序生成的地下城，你参加了六方的一方。然后有单纪元城，这是你在一个大空间见面的地方。这给了我们一个不同的游戏。“
 Meilich表示，游戏行业主要坐在侧链，并在区间的研发。他们似乎对NFTS的公共关系风险有很多恐惧，并且未能以正确的方式实施它们。
 “每个人都在等待某人来推出一个质量的游戏，以及他们可以仰视的东西是这项技术的良好展示，”他说。 “我们正在努力这样做。但与此同时，一旦您获得了100,000或20万人的每月活跃用户，我们希望将一些难以解决的数据汇总了解您获得的此货币溢价。我们正在用这项技术养育（吃自己的狗狗）。“ 
游戏团队的学分包括游戏历史中的一些最好的爱人特许经营，包括Fortnite，战争之神，使命召唤和纵向。作为一家偏远的公司允许团队将他们不同的激情与令人兴奋的风格和普遍主题建立游戏世界。创始团队包括Matt Tonks（两周，战争齿轮）作为首席技术官; Carlo Arellano（魔兽世界，战争之神）作为艺术主任; Brian Alexander（纵向2，蔑视）作为铅作家; TJ STAMB（使命召唤，荣誉勋章）​​作为铅级设计师;和汤姆赵（图例联盟，天空：灯的孩子）作为铅概念艺术家。
 Windows PC正在进行大型时间的发展，这是今年晚些时候迄今为止的宣布。这款游戏预计将于2022年推出。
 “当我们在明年初开始在早期访问时启动游戏时，我们还应该准备开始船上第三方工作室融合这项技术，”梅希说。
 大时间工作室还将开始投资资金进入即将推出的中级PC游戏公司。它将为他们提供作为它们集成NFT技术的激励措施。该团队现在大约有25人。所有远程分发。
   玩家将能够在游戏中获得或找到高价值的物品，就像在地牢中杀死最终老板时一样。但首先，它将从他们可以购买相对较低的碎片。现在，Meilich表示，大时间的工作室不会推出自己的加密货币令牌，因为它不希望与其可能的分销伙伴将使用的东西冲突。 Meilich表示，该公司不会专注于“支付赢得赢得”NFT，其中玩家可以使用他们获得胜利。相反，它将仅销售化妆品，而不是为您提供游戏奖金的物品。
 “对于很多多人游戏，我们看到化妆品，皮肤是真正推动经济的东西，”梅里奇说。 “如果你增加实际稀缺和交易这些资产的能力，我认为它将增加燃料进入人们购买更多物品并收集它们的迫使。所以这就是为什么早期，我们选择在一个没有任何支付的比赛上工作，并且物品纯粹是化妆品的地方，并将提供额外的优势，因为你有更多的钱。“
 技术建立在国内之上，它具有智能合同（可编程性），良好的安全性和主流采用。该公司将存储在自己的数据库中创建或购买的物品的记录，并不会自动在区块链上产生NFT，以便保持成本 - 例如以外地气体费用，或者计算能源的计算成本 - 低，Meilich表示。他认为比特币是一个选择的东西，因为人们保持它而不是与它交易。此外，基础设施并未快速发展。 
用户在以外人上有很多资金，NFTS的价值是球员将能够将它们带到游戏之外并使用它们或卖掉它们。他们可以缩小它们 - 将NFT分成部分，以便购买价格可以更便宜。 Meilich表示，Ethereum支持NFT市场更好，因为越来越多的人可以在它上购买。但如果他们在游戏中使用NFT或以其他方式与他们交易，则玩家将不必招致费用。玩家可以将物品存放在专利的关联保管系统中。它是一个私有数据库，其中NFT和常规项目都可以共存。
  “这允许我们创建托管钱包并将它们返回给用户，”他说。 “Vault的用户体验几乎就像在任何RPG中进行库存一样。玩家可以选择将资产从秘边送到任何时间点。“
 Vault通过常用界面（如Microsoft的PlayFab）连接到游戏的后端。
 “整合并以这样的方式建造它是非常容易的，这是一种禁止的后端，”梅里奇说。
 如果他们想卖掉它们或把它们带到平台之外，那么他们必须薄荷和燃料费用。
 “任何游戏开发人员都可以将其集成到他们的后端。所以这是它的一方。可以在不支付燃气成本前期的情况下施用物品。因此，这是以经济可行的方式完成的，我们会处理资产的监护权。没有更多的人失去私钥的风险，这是我与Decentraland反复遇到的问题，人们会花费数百或数千美元，因为他们失去了私钥，他们会非常沮丧。我们真的没有任何东西可以帮助我们帮助。“
 在这种情况下，大时间的工作室要求用户与其物品的安全相信它。有些人可能会反对那个Ethereum使用很多能量。 Meilich注意到如何制造清洁剂，例如购买碳信用额以抵消能源成本。在某些时候，以太塞将以更好的形式验证，远离与大量采矿和计算成本相关的工作证明，持股权证明，这利用更少的能量。 
Meilich说他不认为这家公司将自己变成一个用户运行的DAO，至少最初，因为它创造了很多额外治理工作。
 长期以来，梅希表示，如果需求实现，该公司将为玩家撤回其资产并将其移到任何区块链。
 “我只是觉得在这一点上的需求就在那里，”他说。
 该公司将首先瞄准北美，以及欧洲，因为它的核心专业知识集中在核心专业知识。
 “我们真的追求一种最大化我们使用平台和分销渠道的机会的方法，”他说。
 在覆盖游戏行业时，Gamesbeat＆＃39; S信条是＆＃34;热情符合业务的地方。＆＃34;这是什么意思？我们想告诉您新闻如何对您而言 - 不仅是在游戏工作室的决策者，而且作为游戏的粉丝。无论您是阅读我们的文章，聆听我们的播客，还是观看我们的视频，GamesBeat将帮助您了解该行业，并享受与之享受。你会怎么做？会员资格包括访问：成为会员
note_en:
Big Time Studios is going big this week with a funding round of $21 million to bring  nonfungible tokens (NFTs) to the biggest game economies.
 The Los Angeles company was started by CEO Ari Meilich, who was previously CEO of  Decentraland, and game designer Thor Alexander.
  The funding comes in two parts: $10.3 million in a round led by FBG Capital as well as North Island Ventures, Digital Currency Group, OKEx Blockdream Ventures, Alameda Research, Circle Financial, and Ashton Kutcher’s Sound Ventures. And Big Time Studios is also in the process of closing an $11 million fund that it will use to invest in game companies that adopt its technology.
 “We are creating all the technology to make it very easy for gaming companies to do this,” Meilich added. “But as a way for us to get to market, we are building the first game, the game. It’s a multiplayer action RPG where you can team up with up to six people and go into dungeons where you kill monsters, some of which are going to be dropping valuable loot. And the way the game is designed, people with zero blockchain experience are going to start collecting NFTs that have real-world value without having to have any previous knowledge about what blockchain technology is about. So ultimately, we’re trying to make the technology underlying technology invisible and just keep the value proposition for the users.”
 Big Time Studios’ team come includes alumni of Epic Games, Riot Games, Blizzard Entertainment, Electronic Arts, and Decentraland. They want to triple-A game companies to adopt cryptocurrency and NFT technology. But to prove that their model works, they are first building their own action role-playing game that will enable players to acquire, hold, and trade NFT-based virtual items without the barriers present in most blockchain applications.
  NFTs are a hot commodity now, driven by the craze around cryptocurrency. NFTs can uniquely identify digital items as authentic and rare through blockchain technology, the transparent and secure digital ledger.   NFTs have exploded in other applications such as art, sports collectibles, and music.  NBA Top Shot (a digital take on collectible basketball cards) is one example. Built by  Dapper Labs, NBA Top Shot has surpassed $500 million in sales, five months after going public. And  an NFT digital collage by the artist Beeple sold at Christie’s for $69.3 million.
 As the founder of Decentraland, Meilich was a pioneer of NFTs. After that company became a decentralized autonomous organization (DAO, run by the owners who bought its tokens), he left and investigated what to do next. He started Big Time Studios in April 2020.
 “After we decentralized the project, Thor and I started working on this new company with the hopes of taking blockchain gaming to a mass-market audience,” Meilich said. “The learnings we’ve had over the past few years is that NFTs seem to have a lot of demand, but from a very niche, very technical audience. Scaling to millions of users — we learned there was no way we could do that with the current user experience that required setting up wallets, having people customize their assets, and perhaps lose them forever if they misplaced their private keys.”
   Investors are pouring money into NFTs, but it’s a good question as to whether NFTs will become a little wave that gets your feet wet in the game industry, or they will be a tidal wave that will sweep through it all. Meilich hopes it will be the latter, but he sees obstacles that have to be overcome. Travis Scher, managing partner at North Island Ventures, said in a statement that the market for in-game assets is massive, but players don’t have the right to own and trade the items they purchase, for the most part. In-game NFTs can give players true ownership and that will bring the metaverse a step closer to fruition, he said.
 “It’s a platform for other gaming companies to introduce NFT’s into their economies,” Meilich said. “We’re starting from the place of the realization that if you look at the Ethereum blockchain, there is a lot of data that shows how much people are spending on NFT’s. For the audience that has been playing first-generation blockchain games, we see that they’ve been spending up to six times more than people do on traditional free-to-play games. It’s a very compelling business case. It’s not only good for the players, because they have more control over their items, but it should also be good for the economy of the game studios, because was the items are transferable.”
 The company’s flagship title is Big Time, an adventure across space and time. It starts at a school at the end of time that is run by headmaster Albert Einstein. The story puts the player in the driver’s seat of their own time machine, allowing them to collect legendary items from amazing historical battles, said Alexander, in an interview with GamesBeat.
 “The gameplay is a combination of fast action gameplay from God of War, mixed up with co-op like in World of Warcraft and Diablo, with a lot of procedural loot systems,” Alexander said. “The world itself is split into three areas. You’ve got your World of Warcraft style, big space where you meet up with other players and show up all your cool loot. Then you’ve got these procedurally generated dungeons that you go into with a party of six. And then there is Epoch City, which is where you meet up in a big space. This gives us a varied set of gameplay.”
 Meilich said the game industry is largely sitting on the sidelines and doing research and development on blockchain. They seem to have a lot of fear about the public relations risks of NFTs and failing to implement them in the right way.
 “Everyone is waiting for someone to come along and launch a game that is of quality, and something that they can look up to that serves as a good showcase for the technology,” he said. “We’re trying to do that. But at the same time, we’re looking to put together some hard data on whether this monetary premium that you get with NFTs is really there, once you get past the 100,000 or 200,000 monthly active users. We’re dogfooding (eating their own dogfood) with the technology.”
 The game team’s credits include some of the best loved franchises in gaming history including Fortnite, God of War, Call of Duty, and Overwatch. Being a remote-first company has allowed the team to combine their diverse passions to build a game world with evocative style and universal themes. The founding team includes Matt Tonks (Fortnight, Gears of War) as chief technology officer; Carlo Arellano (World of Warcraft, God of War) as art director; Brian Alexander (Overwatch 2, Defiance) as lead writer; TJ Stamm (Call of Duty, Medal of Honor) as lead level designer; and Tom Zhao (League of Legends, Sky: Children of the Light) as lead concept artist.
 Development on Big Time is currently underway for Windows PC, with an announcement for the beta coming later this year. The game is expected to launch in 2022.
 “By the time we launch the game in early access in early next year, we should also be ready to start onboarding third-party studios to integrate the technology,” Meilich said.
 Big Time Studios will also start investing money into mid-tier PC game companies that are about to launch. It will provide them with checks as incentives for them to integrate the technology for NFTs. The team has about 25 people now. all remotely distributed.
   Players will be able to earn or find high-value items in the game, like when you kill a final boss in a dungeon. But first it will start with pieces of land that they can buy for relatively low amounts. Right now, Meilich said Big Time Studios won’t launch its own cryptocurrency token because it doesn’t want that to conflict with something that its possible distribution partners would use. Meilich said the company won’t focus on “pay to win” NFTs where players can use them to gain victory. Rather, it will sell only cosmetic items, not the items that give you gameplay bonuses.
 “For a lot of multiplayer games, we see cosmetic items, skins, are what really drives the economy,” Meilich said. “If you add actual scarcity and the ability to trade those assets, I think it’s going to add fuel into the compulsion of people to buy more items and to collect them. So that’s why early on, we chose to work on a game that would not have any pay-to-win component, and where the items were purely cosmetic, and will provide no like extra advantage because you have more money.”
 The tech is built on top of Ethereum, which has both smart contracts (programmability), good security, and mainstream adoption. The company will store records of items created or purchased in its own database, and it won’t automatically mint the NFTs on the blockchain in order to keep costs — such as Ethereum Gas fees, or computing costs that consume energy — low, Meilich said. He didn’t think Bitcoin was much of an option as people are keeping it and not transacting with it. Plus, the infrastructure isn’t developing as fast.
 Users have a lot of funds on Ethereum, and the value of the NFTs is that players will be able to take them outside of the game and use them or sell them. They could fractionalize them — divide NFTs into parts so that the purchase price can be cheaper. Meilich said that Ethereum supports NFT marketplaces better, as more people are available to make purchases on it. But players won’t have to incur fees if they use the NFTs in a game or otherwise don’t transact with them. Players can store the items in an off-chain custody system that is patented. It’s a private database where both NFTs and regular items can coexist.
  “This allows us to create hosted wallets and give them back to users,” he said. “The user experience of the vault is pretty much like having an inventory in any RPG. Players have the option to bring an asset from offchain to onchain at any point in time.”
 The vault connects to the backend of a game through common interfaces such as Microsoft’s Playfab.
 “It’s very easy to integrate and we built it in such a way that it is backend agnostic,” Meilich said.
 If they want to sell them or take them outside the platform, then they have to mint them and incur Gas fees.
 “Any game developer will be able to integrate it to their back end. So that’s one side of it. The items can be minted without paying the gas cost upfront. So this is done in a way that is economically viable, and we take care of the custody of the assets. There’s no more risk of people losing their private key, which is a problem that I’ve encountered repeatedly with Decentraland, where people will spend hundreds or thousands of dollars, and they will be very upset because they lost the private key. And there was really nothing we could do on our end to help.”
 In this case, Big Time Studios is asking users to trust it with security for their items. Some people may object that Ethereum uses a lot of energy. Meilich noted ways to make it cleaner, such as the purchasing of carbon credits to offset the energy costs. At some point, Ethereum will move to a better form of verification, away from proof of work associated with heavy mining and computing costs, toward proof of stake, which uses much less energy.
   Meilich said he doesn’t think the company will turn itself into a user-run DAO this time, at least not initially, as it creates a lot of extra governance work.
 In the long term, Meilich said that if the demand materializes, the company will create ways for players to withdraw their assets and move them to any blockchain.
 “I just don’t feel that the demand is there at this point in time,” he said.
 The company will target North America first, as well as Europe, as that is where its core expertise is focused.
 “We are really going after an approach that maximizes our chances of working with platforms and distribution channels,” he said.
 GamesBeat's creed when covering the game industry is "where passion meets business." What does this mean? We want to tell you how the news matters to you -- not just as a decision-maker at a game studio, but also as a fan of games. Whether you read our articles, listen to our podcasts, or watch our videos, GamesBeat will help you learn about the industry and enjoy engaging with it.  How will you do that? title: 由Decentraland的前首席执行官开始的大时间工作室，以2100万美元的价格出来的隐身，让游戏工作室在他们的游戏中提供NFTS
title_en: Big Time Studios, started by ex-CEO of Decentraland, comes out of stealth with $21M to let game studios offer NFTs in their games

“我不是真正期待他们所做的事情，”莎拉说。 “没有返回第39条。”莎拉的母亲是一个Qanon信徒，他们首先遇到了YouTube上的阴谋理论。现在YouTube已经采取了措施来规范错误信息和阴谋理论，一个新的网站隆隆声已经上升到占据它的位置。莎拉觉得平台让她的母亲远离她。
 莎拉说，隆隆声是“只是关于YouTube的最糟糕的事情，就像100％一样，”Sarah说。 （她的名字已经改变以保护她的身份。）今年早些时候，她的母亲要求在她最喜欢的保守内容创造者（从唐纳德特朗普Jr.到“Patriot Streetfighter”）植入该网站时的帮助访问隆隆声。 Sarah很快成为了支持小组Qanon伤亡人数的150,000名成员之一，因为她的母亲在危险的阴谋理论兔洞进一步下来。
 2020年9月20日至2021年1月，月度网站访问隆隆率从500万到1.15亿;截至4月，他们坐在超过8100万超过8100万。 Sarah的母亲是这些新的隆隆率之一，并且根据莎拉的说法，现在拒绝获得Covid-19疫苗。莎拉说，她的母亲表示，她的母亲表示，她的母亲在隆隆声上发现了许多视频中发现的危险的反vax虚假信息。
 Ellie House是一家关于科技和公司的英国调查记者写作;她以前曾与私人眼睛，BBC世界服务和投资者纪事合作。 Alice Wright是一名基于伦敦的调查记者，涵盖政治和环境问题;她为私人眼睛，时代，前景和其他人写了。 Isabelle Stanley是一个调查记者，涵盖了社会正义问题，用于包括星期日时间和束缚时间。
 隆隆声称它不会促进错误信息或阴谋理论，但只需对法规进行自由言语方式。然而，我们的研究表明，隆隆声不仅允许误导才能在其平台上茁壮成长，它也积极推荐它。
  如果您在隆隆声中搜索“疫苗”，则建议包含关于冠状病毒的误导的视频的可能性比准确信息更多的视频。用户Tommybx的一个视频，特色Carrie Makej-A中的一个流行的声音，在反vax世界声明中，“这不仅仅是一个疫苗;我们正在与人工智能相连。“其他人毫不疑问地说明疫苗是致命的，尚未得到妥善测试。
  即使您在根据我们的研究搜索不相关的术语，即根据我们的研究，您也可能被推荐的Covid-19错误信息，而不是 - 大约一半的建议内容是误导性的。如果您搜索“选举”，您可能是建议错误信息的两倍，而不是事实内容。 
这些发现背后的数据在2月2021年2月收集了五天。使用Guillaume Chaslot首次开发的代码（在YouTube算法上工作的前谷歌员工），收集了哪些视频推荐五个中立的信息：“民主”，“选举”，“法律”，“冠状病毒”和“疫苗”。代码在不同时间的不同日子上为每个单词运行五次，以便数据反映了隆隆的一致推荐算法。
 手动分析了超过6,000个建议。可能有关于什么可以且不能被归类为错误信息，因此这一调查在谨慎方面巧妙。例如，如果内容创建者表示“我不会拿疫苗，因为我认为它可能是一个跟踪芯片，”视频没有作为错误信息分类。虽然如果媒体上规定的视频“疫苗中有一个跟踪装置，”它是。我们的结论是保守的。
 在使用的五个搜索条目中，隆隆声更有可能推荐包含“疫苗，”选举“和”法律“的错误信息的视频。即使对于另外两个词“民主”和“冠状病毒”，推荐误导视频的可能性仍然很高。
  在全世界超过300万人死亡后，这一数据差不多到了大流行，使病毒是假的超过300万人。在隆隆声中寻找“冠状病毒”可能会在大流行的开始时产生更多的错误信息。
  推荐算法在确定用户观看时发挥着重要作用。根据德克萨斯大学培养师媒体参与中心的宣传研究主任Samuel Woolley，算法“倾向于引导人们对极端主义内容的不成比例。”视频流媒体网站经常促进误导和阴谋理论，因为它是有利可图的。 “收入与人们在线度过的时间直接联系起来，”Chaslot说。这是“喜欢海洛因”。并且大多数极端或误导性账户的增长都是从这些网站推广 - “用户和创建者对算法进行零控制。”莎拉认为，YouTube的推荐系统将她的母亲推出了Qanon阴谋理论，现在隆隆声正在保持她的迷路。
 当被问及评论时，一名隆隆声发言人通过电子邮件写道，“隆隆声”禁止煽动暴力，非法内容，种族主义，抗溃疡主义，促进恐怖主义群体（由美国和加拿大政府指定），以及违反版权，以及违反版权许多其他限制。“
 隆隆声称它是Bipartisan，但它选择了2021年保守的政治行动会议首次亮相新的LivestReam工具，主题演讲者唐纳德特朗普重申了他赢得2020年的选举。隆隆声也有强大的支持者，具有深深的保守意见和高调的右翼影响者，他们寻求使用该网站传播实际上不准确的内容。其中一家公司的主要投资者是流行的影响者Dan Bongino，纽约时报列表作为错误信息。 
Chris Pavlovski，Rumble的创始人和首席执行官，使他在社交媒体上市的保守倾向。最近的推文直接与保守思想家和评论员进行了直接聘用，例如争议教授Jordan Peterson和宪法律师Alan Dershowitz。当前国会议员罗恩保罗被YouTube融入了撒谎的医疗错误信息时，Pavlovski使用了他的推特账户要求他加入隆隆声。该公司的官方账户经常转发Pavlovski的个人推文，暗示他的意见与隆隆声的商业方向之间密切的关系。
 虽然隆隆的公司账户是私人的，但似乎从远离YouTube的保守内容创作者的转变时兴起。 11月，Pavlovski告诉财富，该公司在经济上是“自我维持”。他经常使用Hashtag #maketheswitch，鼓励用户将他们的内容移动到他的网站上。 1月份，在国会大厦骚乱之后，隆隆声向Youtube的母公司谷歌提出了20亿美元的反垄断诉讼，声称搜索引擎有目的地促进Youtube上面的隆隆声的视频。 （谷歌否认它通过隆隆声为“毫无索赔”。）

 无论隆隆声的官方政策，该网站的内容和主动建议算法对莎拉等人的生活产生负面影响。然而，关于如何在互联网上规范信息的辩论肆虐。
 Woolley说，像隆隆声和定位者这样的网站不能，不能只是从互联网上脱离，而是认为，联合国和北约等政府和组织应该在具体问题方面“。如果网站主持极端主义内容或允许选举禁令，那么“那么这些就是他们应该规范和诉讼的东西。”
 互及数字仇恨中心首席执行官Imran Ahmed支持更加简单的方法。 “你可以说出你想要的任何东西，但你不能在你想要的地方说出来，”他说，“，”自由自由就像言论自由一样基本。人们有权说'我不想和你做生意。“到最后，他补充说，＆＃34;法规即将到来，所有社交媒体公司都接受了这一点。” 
伍德利说，反规定倡导“自由言论”防守，采取“乌托邦观点的通信存在。”他说，“许多这些数字平台声称所有关于”自由演讲“的人都是基于一个错误的前提，因为始终存在言论。”
 “互联网已被规范化并由强大的实体共同选择，”伍尔利补充道。 “这不是它只是一个基层组织。”例如，Parler来自Mercer系列的资金，突出的美国保守党员参与剑桥分析器。 “当你开始解压缩一些资金和电力结构时，你开始意识到互联网不是这些人吹捧的空间，”伍尔利说。
 Chaslot同意“审查或删除视频是适得其反的，因为它将[那些具有极端视图的人]到其他平台。”为了打击在线误导，YouTube和隆隆声象的网站应该对其算法更加透明。“ Chaslot的Algotranspary组织继续为此进行竞争，但与此同时，他说“我们需要规定”。
  引用了在1月6日在美国国会大厦举起并贡献的放牧和隆隆声并贡献的错误信息，Chaslot补充说，“建议不中立;你有一个股份。“
